## Overview

An `FSItem` is a mostly opaque object, which your file system implementation defines as needed.

The [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes] class defines nonatomic properties to support `FSItem` instances. An [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes] instance contains a snapshot of the attributes of an `FSItem` at one point in time. The [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes] properties have no explicit thread safety provisions, since the operations that either get or set these properties enforce thread safety.

You test an attribute’s validity with the the method [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes/isValid(_:)]. If the value is `true` (Swift) or `YES` (Objective-C), it’s safe to use the attribute.

Methods that get or set an item’s attribute use [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/GetAttributesRequest] or [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/SetAttributesRequest], respectively. Both are subclasses of [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes]. An [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/GetAttributesRequest] contains a [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/GetAttributesRequest/wantedAttributes] property to indicate the attributes a file system provides for the request. Similarly, [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/SetAttributesRequest] uses the property [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/SetAttributesRequest/consumedAttributes] for a file system to signal back which attributes it successfully used.

`FSItem` is the FSKit equivelant of a vnode in the kernel. For every FSKit vnode in the kernel, the `FSModule` hosting the volume has an instantiated `FSItem`.

# FSItem

**Class**

A distinct object in a file hierarchy, such as a file, directory, symlink, socket, and more.

## Declaration

```swift
class FSItem
```

## Overview

An `FSItem` is a mostly opaque object, which your file system implementation defines as needed.

The [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes] class defines nonatomic properties to support `FSItem` instances. An [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes] instance contains a snapshot of the attributes of an `FSItem` at one point in time. The [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes] properties have no explicit thread safety provisions, since the operations that either get or set these properties enforce thread safety.

You test an attribute’s validity with the the method [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes/isValid(_:)]. If the value is `true` (Swift) or `YES` (Objective-C), it’s safe to use the attribute.

Methods that get or set an item’s attribute use [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/GetAttributesRequest] or [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/SetAttributesRequest], respectively. Both are subclasses of [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/Attributes]. An [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/GetAttributesRequest] contains a [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/GetAttributesRequest/wantedAttributes] property to indicate the attributes a file system provides for the request. Similarly, [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/SetAttributesRequest] uses the property [doc://FSKit/documentation/FSKit/FSItem/SetAttributesRequest/consumedAttributes] for a file system to signal back which attributes it successfully used.

`FSItem` is the FSKit equivelant of a vnode in the kernel. For every FSKit vnode in the kernel, the `FSModule` hosting the volume has an instantiated `FSItem`.

## Identifying an item

- **FSItem.Identifier**: The unique identifier for an item.
- **FSItem.ItemType**: An enumeration of item types, such as file, directory, or symbolic link.

## Working with attributes

- **FSItem.Attributes**: Attributes of an item, such as size, creation and modification times, and user and group identifiers.
- **FSItem.GetAttributesRequest**: A request to get attributes from an item.
- **FSItem.SetAttributesRequest**: A request to set attributes on an item.

## Inherits From

- NSObject

## Conforms To

- CVarArg
- CustomDebugStringConvertible
- CustomStringConvertible
- Equatable
- Hashable
- NSObjectProtocol
